title: Uncertainty Quantification using Variational Inference for Biomedical Image Segmentation

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## UNCERTAINTY QUANTIFICATION USING VARIATIONAL INFERENCE FOR BIOMEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Deep learning motivated by convolutional neural networks has been highly successful in a range of medical imaging problems like image classification, image segmentation, image synthesis etc. However for validation and interpretability, not only do we need the predictions made by the model but also how confident it is while making those predictions. This is important in safety critical applications for the people to accept it. In this work, we used an encoder decoder architecture based on variational inference techniques for segmenting brain tumour images. We evaluate our work on the publicly available BRATS dataset using Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and Intersection Over Union (IOU) as the evaluation metrics. Our model is able to segment brain tumours while taking into account both aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty in a principled bayesian manner.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Medical image segmentation is a challenging task for medical practitioners. It is costly, takes time and is prone to error. Hence there is a need to automate the manually done segmentation. Lately Neural Networks have shown great potential on a variety of medical image segmentation problems. The challenge with the approaches used in literature is that the model doesn’t predict the uncertainty associated. This is where Bayesian methods come into play as it gives a principled way of measuring uncertainty from the model predictions. Measuring uncertainty in the output predictions made by neural networks is important for interpretation and validation. Rather than learning the point estimates, Bayesian Neural Networks (BNN) learns the distribution over the weights. The training process of BNN involves first initializing the parameters of the neural network. Next the weights are sampled from some distribution (like gaussian with zero mean and unit variance) and both the forward pass and backward pass is done to update the weights using the conventional backpropagation algorithm.

Monte Carlo dropout networks (Kingma et al., 2015) use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes which still lack theoretical understanding. Bayesian Convolutional Neural Network (Gal and Ghahramani, 2015) use variational inference to learn the posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset. The problem with this approach is that it requires a lot of computation involving a lot of parameters, making this technique not scalable in practice. Variational Autoencoder (Kingma et al., 2015) which is based on generative models solves the above problems and has been successful in a number of tasks like generating images, texts, recommender systems etc. This approach comes with several challenges in its own right which have been successfully tackled in the literature. A random variable sampled from posterior distribution has no gradient so the conventional backpropagation techniques can’t be applied to it. Local Reparameterization Trick (Kingma et al., 2015) was proposed to tackle this by converting the random variable to a deterministic one for computation. The second challenge was the huge computational requirement since it required weight updates in every iteration. Bayes by Backprop algorithm (Blundell et al., 2015) tackled this by calculating gradients in backpropagation using a scale and shift approach by updating the posterior distribution in the backward pass.

## 2 RELATED WORK

## 2.1 MEDICAL IMAGE SEGMENTATION

The problem of segmenting medical images have been successfully tackled in literature using mainly two techniques, first using a Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) (Long et al., 2015) and second those which are based on U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015). The main characteristic of FCN architectures is that it doesn’t use fully connected layers at the end which have been used successfully for image classification problems. U-Net methods on the other hand uses an encoder-decoder architecture with pooling layers in the encoder and upsampling layers in the decoder. Skip connections connect the encoder layers to the decoder layer to create an additional path for the flow of gradients back in the backpropagation step. This helps in reducing overfitting due to many parameters involved while training the network.

## 2.2 BAYESIAN NEURAL NETWORK

Lately, there has been a revival of interest in bayesian methods as some of the inherent problems with deep learning could be solved using it. It is a scalable approach of avoiding overfitting in neural networks and at the same time gives us a measure of uncertainty. This is very important in critical applications where not only do we require the predictions made from the model, but also how confident it is while making its predictions. BNN can be considered as an ensemble of neural networks (Gal, 2016). It has two advantages over the standard neural networks, first it avoids overfitting and second it gives a measure of uncertainty involved.
Instead of point estimates, the neural network learns posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset as defined in Equation 1.
The predictive distribution can be calculated by approximating the integral as defined in Equation 2.

The challenge is that the posterior is often intractable in nature. To combat this, (Neal, 1993) used Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) for learning the weights over the bayesian neural networks. Also (Graves, 2011), (Blundell et al., 2015) and (Louizos and Welling, 2016) proposed independently a technique using variational inference techniques for approximating the posterior distribution. KL Divergence between the posterior and the true distribution can be calculated using Equation 3.
Alternatively minimizing the KL divergence can be written in another form by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) which is tractable. This is shown in Equation 4.

## 2.3 VARIATIONAL INFERENCE

Variational inference finds the parameters of the distribution by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound. ELBO consists of sum of two terms Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between two distributions and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) as defined in Equation 5.

The KL divergence is defined in equation 6.
The posterior in the above equation contains an integral which is intractable in nature. The equation can be re written in Equation 7.

The above equation can be decomposed into two parts one of which is the KL divergence between the exact posterior and its variational approximation which needs to be minimized and the second is ELBO term which needs to be maximized. This is shown in Equation 8.

KL divergence is zero if exact posterior is equal to variational approximation. Since the KL divergence is always greater than zero hence the equation can be approximated by maximizing only the ELBO (Kingma et al., 2015) as defined in equation 9.

## 2.4 ALEATORIC UNCERTAINTY AND EPISTEMIC UNCERTAINTY

There are two types of uncertainty - aleatory and epistemic uncertainty where variance is the sum of both these. Bayesian Neural Networks can be considered an ensemble of neural networks initialized randomly which averages the test results in parallel (Gal, 2016). For final predictions, single mean and variance can be estimated as shown in Equation 10 and Equation 11 respectively.

The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty. Bayesian Neural Network model for uncertainty estimation was done by (Kendall and Gal, 2017) with the last layer representing the mean and variance of logits. The predictive distribution approximating the posterior distribution which gives a measure of uncertainty is defined in Equation 12.

Aleatoric uncertainty is a measure of the variability of the predictions from the dataset hence it is inherent in the data present. The aleatoric uncertainty is the uncertainty that exists inside the dataset. It captures noise inherent in the observations, which arises from the distribution of data. It is highly dependent on bias and distribution inside the input data but not the size of training samples.

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Epistemic uncertainty on the other hand is a measure of the variability of predictions from the model which is tied to various metrics used for evaluation like accuracy, loss etc. It represents the uncertainty inside the model. The epistemic uncertainty will decrease with enough training samples. It is resulting from the limitation of knowledge and data of the system.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 DATASET

To validate the performance of our proposed approach to generalization, publicly available datasets were used for brain tumour segmentation BRATS18 (Menze et al., 2015) and (Bakas et al., 2018). It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma. The images were of resolution 240 _×_ 240 _×_ 155 pixels. The ground truth labels were created by expert neuroradiologists. The sample from the dataset is shown in Figure 2.

Figure 1: Example of MRI slices and ground truth segmentation

## 3.2 DATA AUGMENTATION

The following data augmentation methods were used to increase the size of dataset:

**1. Rescaling:** We rescale the pixels values by rescaling factor 1/255.

**2. Rotation:** Random rotations with the setup degree range between [0, 360] were used.
**3. Height and Width Shift:** Shifting the input to the left or right and up or down was performed.
**4. Shearing Intensity:** It refers to shear angle (unit in degrees) in counter-clockwise direction.

**5. Brightness:** It uses a brightness shift value from the setup range.

## 3.3 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

The prior distribution helps to incorporate learning of the weights over the network. Variational Autoencoder has been used successively as a kind of generative model by sampling from the prior distribution in the encoder. The decoder uses the mean vector and standard deviation vector from the latent space to reconstruct the input. Our model uses a similar encoder decoder architecture as that used in VAEs with the input to the encoder coming from a pre trained image segmentation architecture. We tried different backbones which have previously enjoyed success and found original U-Net gave the best results. The input to the encoder only needs the mean vector, the standard deviation vector of the conditional distribution expressing the confidence with which the pixels are correctly predicted. After passing through the encoder, the parameters get converted to a latent representation which is again sampled in a mean and standard deviation vector. The decoder later recovers this back to the original distribution. The conventional backpropagation algorithm is used for training the model with gradient descent. The network architecture is shown in Figure 1.

## 3.5 LOSS FUNCTIONS

A combination of binary cross entropy and dice losses have been used to train the network. The first part binary cross entropy is a commonly used loss function for classification problems as shown by (Goodfellow et al., 2016). Every pixel in the image needs to be classified and hence loss function can be written as shown in Equation 18.

The problem with binary cross entropy loss is that it doesn’t take into account the class imbalance as the background is the dominant class. This is one of fundamental challenges in semantic segmentation problems. Dice Loss is robust to the aforementioned problem which is based on Dice Similarity Coefficient as defined in Equation 19.
Both the loss terms were combined in a single term with more weight given to the Dice Loss term since it handles the class imbalance problem better. This is shown in Equation 20.

## 3.6 EVALUATION METRICS

Evaluation metrics for semantic segmentation problems which have often been used in literature are Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) also known as F1-score and Intersection over union (IoU). The corresponding equations are shown in Equation 16 and Equation 17 respectively.

True positive (TR), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP) number of pixels is calculated separately for each image and averaged over the test set. The ground truth is labelled manually by experts which are compared against.

## 4 RESULTS

Figure 3 displays how accuracy (y-axis) behaves based on how much the data (x-axis) holds.

**1. MC dropout:** This is a method based on Bayesian Neural Networks and activated dropout function at inference time. This can be defined using the below equation:

**2. Deep ensembles:** We collect the results of each image trained on five models and compute the average mean. We then calculate the entropy of these mean values as the uncertainty value on each image.

The darker(dull) color denotes more confidence while the lighter(brighter) color means the model is less confident while segmenting those areas. Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we proposed a way to quantify uncertainty in the context of medical image segmentation. Our network is based on an encoder decoder framework similar to that used by VAEs. The weights of the network represent distributions instead of point estimates and thus give a principled way of measuring uncertainty at the same time while making the predictions. Our model uses bayesian neural networks for both the encoder and decoder. The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly. We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a clinically important problem—uncertainty quantification in biomedical image segmentation—and aims to integrate variational inference into a U-Net-style architecture. It correctly identifies key uncertainty types (aleatoric and epistemic) and uses a Bayesian framework, which is conceptually appropriate. The use of the BRATS dataset and standard segmentation metrics aligns with community practices.

Overview issues:

- No empirical validation of uncertainty calibration: The paper claims to provide principled Bayesian uncertainty estimates but does not evaluate their calibration using standard metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) or reliability diagrams. Table 2 reports raw uncertainty values but offers no analysis of whether high uncertainty correlates with actual segmentation errors, especially at tumor boundaries. Without such evaluation, the claimed 'principled' uncertainty quantification lacks empirical support. This is a fundamental shortcoming in safety-critical applications where overconfident predictions can be dangerous. The authors must demonstrate that their uncertainty maps are meaningful and well-calibrated, for instance by plotting error vs. uncertainty curves or computing ECE on segmentation outputs.

- Incorrect formulation of the variational objective: Equation 13 presents a loss function that does not correspond to the standard ELBO used in variational inference. The expression combines terms from the encoder and decoder without clear connection to the marginal likelihood, and the notation (e.g., summing over model parameters as if they were data points) is mathematically incoherent. The objective appears to conflate VAE-style reconstruction with Bayesian weight regularization. This undermines the theoretical foundation of the method. The authors should derive the correct ELBO for a Bayesian segmentation network, clearly distinguishing between likelihood, prior, and variational posterior terms, and align their training objective accordingly.

- Missing comparison to established uncertainty baselines: Despite listing MC Dropout and deep ensembles in Section 4, the paper does not compare segmentation performance or uncertainty quality against these methods. The uncertainty values in Table 2 are reported for different models but without corresponding segmentation accuracy or error-localization performance. Readers cannot determine whether the proposed method produces better or more reliable uncertainty than simpler alternatives. Given that deep ensembles often outperform VI in calibration, a direct comparison on both DSC and uncertainty metrics is essential for establishing the method’s value.

- Uncertainty maps lack semantic or clinical interpretation: Figure 4 shows uncertainty visualizations but does not analyze where high uncertainty occurs—e.g., whether it aligns with tumor boundaries, ambiguous regions, or known sources of inter-rater variability. The claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are 'correlated' is vague and unsupported by quantitative analysis. In biomedical segmentation, the utility of uncertainty depends on its ability to flag clinically relevant errors. The authors should overlay uncertainty heatmaps with expert disagreement maps or known difficult regions (e.g., edema infiltration) to demonstrate clinical relevance.

- Method section omits critical architectural and inference details: Section 3 is entirely missing, and the network description in Section 3.3 is vague and misleading. It references a pre-trained segmentation backbone but does not specify how variational inference is integrated into U-Net layers. It is unclear whether weight distributions are placed on convolutional kernels, and how the 'encoder' and 'decoder' in Figure 2 relate to the segmentation network. The algorithm uses latent variables over weights, but no justification is given for this global approximation. A detailed architecture diagram and specification of where stochasticity is introduced (e.g., mean-field layers) are necessary for reproducibility and credibility.

- Evaluation conflates segmentation accuracy with uncertainty quality: The results focus entirely on DSC and IoU, which measure segmentation accuracy but not uncertainty performance. The paper does not test whether higher uncertainty predicts lower accuracy on a per-sample or per-region basis. For instance, a simple ablation could show whether regions with top-20% epistemic uncertainty have significantly higher Dice error. Without such analysis, the paper fails to demonstrate that the computed uncertainties are useful. The evaluation must include uncertainty-specific metrics such as AUROC for error detection or correlation between uncertainty and local Dice.

- No calibration analysis of uncertainty estimates: The paper claims to provide principled Bayesian uncertainty quantification but does not evaluate whether the reported uncertainty values are well-calibrated. In safety-critical applications like medical segmentation, it is essential to show that high uncertainty correlates with high error rates. The current results report raw uncertainty values in Table 2 but do not link them to segmentation performance. Readers cannot tell if uncertain predictions are actually inaccurate. The authors should compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE) for segmentation outputs and plot reliability diagrams that bin predictions by uncertainty level and show the corresponding error rate. This would demonstrate that uncertainty has practical meaning and supports the paper's central claim of reliable uncertainty estimation.

- No evaluation of uncertainty under domain shift: Biomedical models often face distributional shifts due to differences in imaging protocols or patient populations. The paper evaluates performance on BRATS but does not test uncertainty behavior under such shifts, which is critical for clinical deployment. For example, a model might remain accurate on in-distribution data but produce overconfident errors on out-of-distribution cases. The authors should simulate domain shift—e.g., by applying intensity perturbations or using data from different BRATS acquisition sites—and evaluate whether uncertainty increases appropriately. This would demonstrate robustness and strengthen the practical relevance of the uncertainty estimates.

- Lack of ablation linking uncertainty to segmentation errors: The paper does not analyze whether high uncertainty regions correspond to actual segmentation mistakes. Figure 4 shows uncertainty maps but provides no quantitative assessment of spatial alignment between uncertainty and error. A natural evaluation would compute the Dice score in regions with high vs. low epistemic uncertainty and test for significant differences. Alternatively, the authors could compute AUROC for detecting misclassified pixels using uncertainty as a predictor. This kind of analysis is standard in uncertainty-aware segmentation and is needed to show that the computed uncertainties are diagnostically useful rather than arbitrary.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Derive and implement a correct variational inference objective aligned with the ELBO for Bayesian neural networks, and clearly specify the model and variational family.

- Evaluate uncertainty calibration using ECE or reliability diagrams, and assess whether uncertainty correlates with segmentation errors, especially at boundaries.

- Compare segmentation and uncertainty performance against MC Dropout and deep ensembles using both accuracy and uncertainty quality metrics.

- Provide a detailed network architecture description, including where stochasticity is introduced and how the U-Net is made Bayesian.

- Analyze uncertainty maps in clinically meaningful contexts, such as tumor margins or regions of known inter-observer variability.

comments.title: Incorrect variational objective in Equation 13
comments.explanation: The objective function in Equation 13 does not correspond to a valid evidence lower bound (ELBO) for Bayesian neural networks. The summation over data index $i$ and per-sample variational parameters $\theta_i$, $\psi^{(i)}$ suggest a separate posterior for each data point, which contradicts the concept of shared network weights. Moreover, the terms involve log-densities of the variational posterior and encoder evaluated at sampled weights, which diverge during optimization. A proper ELBO should be $\mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(w)}[\log p(\mathcal{D}|w)] - \mathrm{KL}(q_\theta(w) \|\| p(w))$, with global $q_\theta(w)$ over shared weights. The current form conflates latent variable modeling with weight-space inference and lacks a coherent probabilistic foundation.

comments.title: Decoder definition breaks generative process
comments.quote: Let dataset be denoted by  $\mathcal{D}:\{(x_i,y_i)\}_{i = 1}^N$ , variational approximation of the posterior distribution by  $q_{\theta}(w)$ , encoder as  $r_{\psi}(z|w)$  and decoder as  $p_{\phi}(w)$ .
comments.explanation: The decoder is defined as $p_\phi(w)$, a distribution over model weights, but in a variational framework the decoder should generate data or latent variables, not weights. This severs the link between the latent code $z$ and the input $x$ or output $y$. If the model intends to place a prior on weights via a latent variable, the decoder should be $p_\phi(w|z)$, not $p_\phi(w)$. As written, there is no mechanism for data reconstruction or segmentation likelihood, making the variational objective incoherent. The definition must be corrected to reflect a proper generative process.

comments.title: Gradient update references undefined image reconstruction
comments.explanation: Equation 15 computes gradients using $\log p_\phi(x^{(k)}|z)$, implying the model reconstructs input images. However, the paper focuses on segmentation, not autoencoding, and no segmentation likelihood $p(y|x,w)$ appears in the objective. The decoder was previously defined as $p_\phi(w)$, which cannot produce $x$. This disconnect suggests the training objective does not optimize segmentation performance. Either the model must include a segmentation loss, or the decoder must be redefined as $p_\phi(x|z)$ with justification for an autoencoding component. As written, the learning signal is misaligned with the task.

comments.title: Per-sample weight sampling breaks model coherence
comments.quote: Sample weights with reparametrization:  $\widehat{w}^{(i)}\sim q_{\theta_i}\left(w^{(i)}\right)$  Sample latent variables with reparametrization:  $\widehat{z}^{(i)}\sim r_{\psi^{(i)}}\left(z|\widehat{w}^{(i)}\right)$
comments.explanation: The algorithm samples weights $\widehat{w}^{(i)}$ per data point $i$ from $q_{\theta_i}(w^{(i)})$, suggesting a distinct posterior for each training example. This contradicts the standard assumption of shared network weights in deep learning. The indexing $\theta_i$, $\psi^{(i)}$ implies non-shared parameters, making the model infeasible for large datasets. Reparametrized sampling should apply to a single global $q_\theta(w)$ over shared weights, sampled once per minibatch. The per-sample indexing must be removed to maintain model coherence and scalability.

comments.title: Incorrect ELBO form in Equation 4
comments.quote: $-\int_{\Omega}q_{\theta}(\omega)\log p(y|x,\omega)d\omega+KL\left\{q_{\theta}(\omega)\|p(\omega)\right\}$ (4)
comments.explanation: Equation 4 presents an invalid ELBO. The first term should be an expectation over the full dataset: $\sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(\omega)}[\log p(y_i|x_i,\omega)]$, not a single $(x,y)$ pair. The current form lacks data dependence and normalization, making it inconsistent with the posterior definition in Equation 1. Additionally, the sign convention is ambiguous—when maximizing, the KL term should be subtracted. Rewrite as $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(\omega)}[\log p(y_i|x_i,\omega)] - \mathrm{KL}(q_\theta(\omega)\|\| p(\omega))$ to reflect the standard derivation.

comments.title: Syntactic error in KL minimization (Equation 5)
comments.quote: $\min\mathrm{KL}\left(q_{\theta}(w)\right)\|p(w|\mathcal{D}))$ (5)
comments.explanation: Equation 5 contains unmatched parentheses: $\mathrm{KL}\left(q_{\theta}(w)\right)$ closes prematurely. The correct form is $\min_\theta \mathrm{KL}\left(q_\theta(w) \|\| p(w|\mathcal{D})\right)$. The optimization variable $\theta$ is also missing from the minimization operator. While the intent is clear, the notation obscures the dependence of $q_\theta$ on $\theta$ and could mislead readers. Correct the syntax and explicitly include $\theta$ in the minimization.

comments.title: KL divergence notation conflates distributions and densities
comments.quote: $\mathrm{KL}(q(x))\|p(x))=-\int q(x)\log\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right)$ (6)
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathrm{KL}(q(x)\|\| p(x))$ is misleading: KL divergence is between distributions $q$ and $p$, not their density evaluations. The use of $x$ here conflicts with later use of $w$ for weights, creating notational discontinuity. Rewrite as $\mathrm{KL}(q \|\| p) = \int q(w) \log\left(\frac{q(w)}{p(w)}\right) dw$ to use consistent variable names and proper distribution-level notation.

comments.title: Undefined term in ELBO decomposition (Equation 9)
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log p(\mathcal{D}|w)-\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log\frac{q_{\theta}(w)}{p(w)}=\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{D}}-\mathrm{KL}\left(q_{\theta}(w)\|p(w)\right)$ (9)
comments.explanation: The term $\mathcal{L}_\mathcal{D}$ is used without definition. From context, it must denote $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log p(\mathcal{D}|w)$, but this should be explicitly stated. The middle expression $-\mathbb{E}\log\frac{q_\theta(w)}{p(w)}$ equals $-\mathrm{KL}(q_\theta(w)\|\| p(w))$, so the full form is correct, but the intermediate step introduces unnecessary algebraic complexity. Define $\mathcal{L}_\mathcal{D}$ and simplify the expression to $\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log p(\mathcal{D}|w) - \mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(w)\|\| p(w))$ for clarity.

comments.title: Incorrect variance decomposition in Equation 11
comments.quote:
$\hat{\sigma}_{c}^{2}(x)=\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\hat{\sigma}_{i}^{2}(x)+\left[\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\hat{\mu}_{i}^{2}(x)-\hat{\mu}^{2}(x)\right]$ (11)

The first term in variance denotes aleatoric uncertainty while the second denotes epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The second term uses $\hat{\mu}^2(x)$ without defining $\hat{\mu}(x)$. If intended to denote $\mu_c(x)$ from Equation 10, the notation is inconsistent. The correct epistemic uncertainty is $\mathrm{Var}_i(\hat{\mu}_i(x)) = \frac{1}{M}\sum \hat{\mu}_i^2(x) - \left(\frac{1}{M}\sum \hat{\mu}_i(x)\right)^2$, which requires squaring the mean, not the individual terms. The current form does not compute a valid variance and misrepresents the uncertainty decomposition. Rewrite with consistent notation and correct algebraic form.

comments.title: Misleading claim about aleatoric-epistemic correlation
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are positively correlated contradicts standard understanding—they are conceptually distinct and not inherently coupled. No quantitative evidence (e.g., correlation coefficient, scatter plot) supports this assertion. Visual similarity in spatial patterns does not imply statistical correlation. The statement should be removed or supported with a pixel-wise correlation analysis from the uncertainty maps in Figure 4.

comments.title: Incorrect BRATS dataset description
comments.quote: It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma.
comments.explanation: The BRATS18 dataset includes 285 cases (210 high-grade, 75 low-grade), not 175. More critically, 'lower grade glioblastoma' is a clinical contradiction—glioblastoma is WHO grade IV, while lower-grade gliomas are grades II–III. The correct phrasing should distinguish between high-grade gliomas (including glioblastoma) and lower-grade gliomas. The current description misrepresents both the dataset size and clinical taxonomy.

comments.title: Redundant rotation augmentation range
comments.quote: Rotation: Random rotations with the setup degree range between [0, 360] were used.
comments.explanation: A rotation range of [0, 360] degrees produces transformations equivalent to smaller angles (e.g., 270° ≡ -90°), offering no effective augmentation beyond [0, 180°]. Standard practice uses symmetric ranges around zero (e.g., [-30°, 30°]) to introduce meaningful variation. The current range likely results in redundant or identity-like transformations, undermining the purpose of data augmentation. Specify a narrow, symmetric range and clarify the sampling method.

comments.title: Ambiguous brightness augmentation parameters
comments.quote: Brightness: It uses a brightness shift value from the setup range.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'setup range' is undefined. No specific bounds or sampling method (additive vs. multiplicative) are provided, making the augmentation irreproducible. Specify the exact range (e.g., 'additive shifts sampled uniformly from [-0.1, 0.1]') and implementation details to ensure replicability.

comments.title: Incorrect Dice loss formulation
comments.explanation: The loss sums $1 - DSC^{(i)}$ over $N$ classes or regions, making it scale with $N$. This is undesirable—performance should not depend on the number of classes. The standard Dice loss is a single scalar: $\mathcal{L}_{DICE} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum \hat{y} y}{\sum \hat{y} + \sum y}$. Additionally, using $TP_i, FP_i, FN_i$ suggests hard predictions, but Dice loss must use soft probabilities for differentiability. Rewrite using continuous outputs and a global formulation.

comments.title: Binary cross-entropy misapplied in multi-class setting
comments.explanation: Equation 16 defines binary cross-entropy, which assumes a single binary label per pixel. In multi-class segmentation (e.g., BRATS), labels are one-hot encoded and require categorical cross-entropy: $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{i,j} \sum_c y_{i,j,c} \log \widehat{y}_{i,j,c}$. The paper does not state whether the task is binary or justify using BCE. Replace with the correct multi-class formulation or clarify the binary nature of the problem.

comments.title: Unjustified fixed weighting in loss combination
comments.explanation: The fixed 0.9/0.1 weights are introduced without normalization or ablation. BCE loss can be orders of magnitude larger than Dice loss, making the weighting ineffective. For example, if $\mathcal{L}_{CE} \approx 10$ and $\mathcal{L}_{DICE} \approx 0.5$, BCE dominates despite its smaller coefficient. Proper combination requires gradient normalization (e.g., GradNorm) or dynamic scaling. Justify the ratio with an ablation study or use a normalized form.

comments.title: Incorrect MC dropout uncertainty formula
comments.quote: $\text{Uncertainty}_{mcd}=-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{c}p_{j}\log p_{j}\right)/M_{i}$ (21)
comments.explanation: The formula is incorrect: the inner sum $\sum_j p_j \log p_j$ does not depend on $i$, so all $M$ terms are identical, reducing the expression to a single entropy computation. The denominator $M_i$ is undefined. The correct form computes the mean predictive distribution $\bar{p}_j = \frac{1}{M} \sum_i p_j^{(i)}$, then entropy $-\sum_j \bar{p}_j \log \bar{p}_j$. Rewrite Equation 21 accordingly.

comments.title: Table reference error in results description
comments.quote: The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly states that Mean Dice Similarity values are in Table 2, but Table 2 contains only uncertainty values. The actual DSC values are in Table 3. Similarly, the next sentence misattributes IoU values to Table 3 when they are in Table 4. Correct the references: '...shown in Table 3' and '...shown in Table 4'.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of input source
comments.quote: The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly.
comments.explanation: This suggests the encoder receives segmentation masks or uncertainty estimates from other models, which contradicts standard practice. The encoder should process raw or preprocessed images, not outputs from pretrained segmentation networks. If features are used, state clearly: 'The encoder takes raw MRI volumes, optionally augmented with features from a pretrained backbone.' Remove any implication that label confidence distributions are inputs.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of state-of-the-art performance
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: The paper presents no comparison to established state-of-the-art methods (e.g., nnU-Net, TransUNet) on BRATS. Tables 3 and 4 report only the proposed model's performance, not baselines. Without such comparisons, the claim of outperforming the state of the art is unsubstantiated. Rephrase as 'achieving competitive performance' or include proper baselines.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a variational inference approach for uncertainty quantification in brain tumor segmentation. While the core idea of using Bayesian methods for medical image segmentation is sound, the paper suffers from significant methodological gaps, unclear technical contributions, and insufficient validation of uncertainty estimates.

The paper attempts to address an important problem in medical imaging - quantifying uncertainty in segmentation predictions. It correctly identifies the need for both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty estimation. However, the method is poorly explained, the variational formulation appears disconnected from the actual implementation, and the evaluation of uncertainty quality is inadequate. The paper reads more like a proof-of-concept than a fully developed research contribution.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Variational Formulation: The paper presents a confusing mix of variational autoencoder concepts and Bayesian neural networks. Equation 13 defines an objective function that appears to be a VAE-style ELBO, but the relationship between this objective and the claimed Bayesian neural network posterior approximation is never clarified. The algorithm describes sampling weights from q_θ(w) and latent variables from r_ψ(z|w), but it's unclear how these relate to standard VI for BNNs. The paper needs to either (a) clearly explain how this is a novel VI formulation for BNNs, or (b) acknowledge it's using a VAE-based approach and clarify the connection to uncertainty quantification.

- Missing Uncertainty Calibration Evaluation: The paper only reports raw uncertainty values (Table 2) without any assessment of whether these uncertainties are calibrated or meaningful. No reliability diagrams, Expected Calibration Error, or other calibration metrics are provided. The claim that darker colors indicate more confidence (Figure 4) is purely qualitative. For a paper focused on uncertainty quantification, demonstrating that the uncertainties are well-calibrated is essential. The authors should add calibration diagnostics and proper scoring rules to validate their uncertainty estimates.

- Inadequate Baseline Comparisons: While the paper mentions MC dropout and deep ensembles as baselines, it only reports their uncertainty values without comparing segmentation performance. The DSC and IOU results (Tables 3-4) are only shown for the proposed method with varying training sizes. There's no comparison of segmentation accuracy against established baselines on the same data splits. The claim of outperforming state-of-the-art in the conclusion is unsupported by the results. The authors need to include fair comparisons of both segmentation performance and uncertainty quality against multiple baselines.

- Ambiguous Uncertainty Decomposition: The paper claims to separate aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty (Figure 4) but provides no mathematical details on how this separation is achieved in their VAE-style framework. Equations 10-11 describe the decomposition for standard BNN ensembles, but it's unclear how this applies to their method. The observation that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are correlated contradicts the typical expectation that they capture different phenomena. The authors need to clarify their decomposition method and verify it correctly separates these uncertainty types.

- Insufficient Methodological Details: Critical implementation details are missing. The network architecture figure (Figure 2) is illegible in the provided PDF. The relationship between the pretrained backbone (U-Net) and the variational encoder-decoder is not explained. It's unclear whether the variational parameters are applied to the backbone weights or represent a separate module. The latent dimension of 10 (Table 1) seems small for encoding entire network weights. The paper needs to provide a clear architectural diagram and explain how the variational inference integrates with the segmentation network.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous uncertainty benefits: The paper never shows that its uncertainty estimates provide practical value beyond what simpler baselines offer. For a method claiming principled uncertainty quantification, there should be at least one experiment demonstrating how these uncertainties help in practice. A natural demonstration would be an active learning experiment where the model's uncertainty guides sample selection, showing that uncertainty-informed sampling outperforms random sampling. Alternatively, the authors could show failure case analysis where high uncertainty regions correlate with segmentation errors, or demonstrate that uncertainty thresholds can flag low-quality predictions. Without such demonstrations, the claimed benefits of uncertainty quantification remain unsubstantiated.

- No worked example of variational posterior computation: The paper presents the variational framework but never computes the posterior quantities for a concrete model. Readers need to see how the variational parameters relate to actual network weights. A minimal example would specify the variational family (e.g., diagonal Gaussian), show how the ELBO gradients are computed for a single layer, and verify that the learned posterior captures weight uncertainty. This could be demonstrated on a simplified 2-layer network or even analytically for a toy problem. Without such grounding, the connection between the abstract formulation and practical implementation remains unclear.

- Underdeveloped computational feasibility analysis: The paper claims to address scalability issues with Bayesian methods but provides no analysis of computational costs. For a method applied to 3D medical images (240×240×155), readers need to know if the approach is feasible in practice. The authors should report training time per epoch, memory requirements, and how these scale with image size and network depth. A comparison of computational costs against MC dropout and deep ensembles would be particularly valuable. Without this analysis, the claim of practical scalability is unsupported.

- Missing verification of uncertainty decomposition correctness: The paper shows aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty maps (Figure 4) but never verifies that these components capture the intended phenomena. A minimal verification would involve controlled experiments: (1) adding synthetic noise to test images should increase aleatoric but not epistemic uncertainty, (2) training on fewer samples should increase epistemic but not aleatoric uncertainty. The authors could also compute the total predictive variance and verify it matches the sum of the two components. Without such validation, the uncertainty decomposition appears arbitrary.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the variational formulation and its connection to Bayesian neural networks, with proper mathematical derivation

- Add comprehensive uncertainty calibration evaluation including reliability diagrams and proper scoring rules

- Include fair baseline comparisons for both segmentation performance and uncertainty quality

- Provide detailed explanation of aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty decomposition in the proposed framework

- Add clear architectural diagrams and implementation details for the proposed method

comments.title: Missing reconstruction term in ELBO
comments.explanation: The objective function in Equation 13 is missing the data likelihood term that should appear in a VAE-style ELBO. A proper ELBO should include E_{q(w,z)}[log p(y|x,w,z)] or similar reconstruction term. Without this term, the objective only contains KL-like terms between distributions but no connection to the actual segmentation task. This makes it unclear how the model learns to perform segmentation rather than just matching weight distributions.

comments.title: Incorrect ELBO sign and missing terms
comments.quote:
.

$-\int_{\Omega}q_{\theta}(\omega)\log p(y|x,\omega)d\omega+KL\left\{q_{\theta}(\omega)\|p(\omega)\right\}$ (4)
comments.explanation: Equation 4 has two critical errors. First, the ELBO should be maximized, but the first term is negative, which would minimize it. The correct ELBO form is $\mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(\omega)}[\log p(y|x,\omega)] - KL\{q_\theta(\omega)\|p(\omega)\}$. Second, the equation is missing the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ and summation over all training samples - it should be $\sum_{i=1}^N \mathbb{E}_{q_\theta(\omega)}[\log p(y_i|x_i,\omega)] - KL\{q_\theta(\omega)\|p(\omega)\}$. This error propagates through the entire variational formulation.

comments.title: Incorrect gradient ascent direction
comments.explanation: Equation 14 shows gradient ascent (adding the gradient) but the text states gradient descent is used. Since the objective function in Equation 13 appears to be a negative log-likelihood based loss that should be minimized, this should be gradient descent with subtraction. The update should be θ = θ - α∇_θ L and ψ = ψ - β∇_ψ L for minimization, or the authors should clarify if they're maximizing a different objective.

comments.title: Incorrect MC dropout uncertainty formula
comments.quote: $\text{Uncertainty}_{mcd}=-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{c}p_{j}\log p_{j}\right)/M_{i}$ (21)
comments.explanation: The formula has a mathematical error in the denominator. Using $M_i$ as the denominator doesn't make sense since $i$ is the summation index variable. The correct formula for average entropy across M MC samples should be: $\text{Uncertainty}_{mcd} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \left(-\sum_{j=1}^{c} p_{ij} \log p_{ij}\right)$. Additionally, there's a notation inconsistency where $p_i$ is mentioned in the text but $p_j$ appears in the formula.

comments.title: Malformed ensemble MC dropout equation
comments.quote: $Uncertainty_{ens-mcd}=-\sum_{k=1}^{D}((\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{c}p_{j}\log p_{j})/M_{i}\ /D_{k}$ (22)
comments.explanation: Equation (22) has multiple mathematical errors: unbalanced parentheses, incorrect use of $M_i$ and $D_k$ as denominators when these are summation indices, and missing closing parenthesis. The correct formula for ensemble MC dropout uncertainty should average the entropy across both M samples and D models: $Uncertainty_{ens-mcd} = \frac{1}{D} \sum_{k=1}^{D} \left(\frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \left(-\sum_{j=1}^{c} p_{ijk} \log p_{ijk}\right)\right)$.

comments.title: Inconsistent optimizer specification
comments.quote:
|  Parameter | Value  |
| --- | --- |
|  Batch Size | 16  |
|  Optimizer | Adam  |
|  Learning Rate | 0.001  |
|  LR scheduler patience | 10  |
|  LR scheduler factor | 0.1  |
|  Latent Variable Size | 10  |
|  Max epochs | 500  |
|  Dropout(Both Training and Test) | 0.3  |
comments.explanation: The text specifies momentum=0.9 for gradient descent, but Table 1 lists Adam as the optimizer. Adam optimizer doesn't use traditional momentum in the same way as SGD with momentum. Adam maintains adaptive learning rates per parameter using first and second moment estimates, with default beta_1=0.9 and beta_2=0.999. The momentum parameter of 0.9 appears to be conflated with Adam's beta_1 parameter. This creates confusion about which optimizer was actually used.

comments.title: Unsupported state-of-the-art claim
comments.quote: We evaluated our network on publicly available BRATS dataset with our model outperforming previous state of the art using DSC and IOU evaluation metrics.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims the model outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods, but the paper's results section (Tables 3-4) only shows DSC and IOU scores for the proposed method with varying training sizes. There are no comparative results against any baseline methods on the same data splits. Without baseline comparisons, the claim of outperforming state-of-the-art is unsubstantiated by the presented results.

comments.title: Incorrect citation attribution for MC Dropout
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks *(Kingma et al., 2015)* use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes which still lack theoretical understanding.
comments.explanation: The citation for Monte Carlo dropout is incorrect. MC Dropout was introduced by Gal and Ghahramani (2016) in 'Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation: Representing Model Uncertainty in Deep Learning' (ICML), not Kingma et al. (2015). Kingma et al. (2015) introduced Variational Autoencoders. This error misattributes a foundational method and could confuse readers about the literature lineage.

comments.title: Incorrect citation attribution for Bayesian CNN
comments.quote: Bayesian Convolutional Neural Network *(Gal and Ghahramani, 2015)* use variational inference to learn the posterior distribution over the weights given the dataset.
comments.explanation: The citation year for Bayesian Convolutional Neural Networks is incorrect. Gal and Ghahramani's work on Bayesian CNNs was published in 2016 (ICML), not 2015. The 2015 citation appears to be another error where the Kingma et al. (2015) VAE paper year was mistakenly applied. This creates inconsistency with the correct 2016 citation that should be used for MC Dropout.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of BNN training process
comments.quote: The training process of BNN involves first initializing the parameters of the neural network. Next the weights are sampled from some distribution (like gaussian with zero mean and unit variance) and both the forward pass and backward pass is done to update the weights using the conventional backpropagation algorithm.
comments.explanation: This description oversimplifies and misrepresents BNN training. In BNNs, we don't sample weights from a fixed prior distribution (like N(0,1)) and then update them with conventional backpropagation. Instead, we learn the parameters of a variational posterior distribution q(w|θ) through gradient-based optimization of the ELBO. The sampling happens during inference or for gradient estimation, not as the core training mechanism.

comments.title: Sign error in ELBO decomposition
comments.quote:
$\mathcal{L}(\theta)=\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log p(\mathcal{D}|w)-\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log\frac{q_{\theta}(w)}{p(w)}=\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{D}}-\mathrm{KL}\left(q_{\theta}(w)\|p(w)\right)$ (9)

#
comments.explanation: The derivation in Equation 9 has a sign error. Starting from the second term: $-\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log\frac{q_{\theta}(w)}{p(w)} = -\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}[\log q_{\theta}(w) - \log p(w)] = -\mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log q_{\theta}(w) + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log p(w)$. This equals $\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(w)\|p(w)) - \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log q_{\theta}(w) + \mathbb{E}_{q_{\theta}(w)}\log q_{\theta}(w) = \mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(w)\|p(w))$, not $-\mathrm{KL}(q_{\theta}(w)\|p(w))$ as shown.

comments.title: Incorrect variance decomposition formula
comments.quote:
$\hat{\sigma}_{c}^{2}(x)=\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\hat{\sigma}_{i}^{2}(x)+\left[\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\hat{\mu}_{i}^{2}(x)-\hat{\mu}^{2}(x)\right]$ (11)

The
comments.explanation: Equation 11 has an incorrect variance decomposition. The second term should be the variance of the means, not the difference between the mean of squared predictions and the squared mean. The correct decomposition is: $\hat{\sigma}_{c}^{2}(x)=\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\hat{\sigma}_{i}^{2}(x)+\frac{1}{M}\sum_{i=1}^{M}(\hat{\mu}_{i}(x)-\hat{\mu}_{c}(x))^{2}$. The current formula is mathematically equivalent but obscures the interpretation: the second term should clearly represent the variance of the predictive means across ensemble members, which is the epistemic uncertainty.

comments.title: Cross entropy loss missing parentheses
comments.explanation: The binary cross entropy loss formula is missing parentheses around the entire expression. The standard binary cross entropy loss should be L_CE = -Σ[y_ij log(ŷ_ij) + (1 - y_ij) log(1 - ŷ_ij)]. The current formula shows L_CE = -Σ y_ij log(ŷ_ij) + (1 - y_ij) log(1 - ŷ_ij), which incorrectly applies the negative sign only to the first term.

comments.title: Dice Loss denominator undefined for empty predictions
comments.quote:
ation 19.

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {D I C E} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {N} \frac {F N _ {i} + F P _ {i}}{2 T P _ {i} + F N _ {i} + F P _ {i}} = \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {N} \left(1 - D S C ^ {(i)}\right) \tag {17}
$$

Bot
comments.explanation: The Dice Loss formula becomes undefined when TP = FN = FP = 0 (empty prediction and ground truth). In this case, the denominator 2TP + FN + FP = 0, leading to division by zero. Add a small epsilon term to the denominator: L_DICE = Σ_{i=1}^N (FN_i + FP_i)/(2TP_i + FN_i + FP_i + ε) where ε > 0 (e.g., ε = 1e-6) to ensure numerical stability.

comments.title: Incorrect description of input sampling
comments.quote: The inputs to encoder come from pre trained backbones like U-Net, V-Net and FCN sampled from conditional distribution representing the confidence with which pixels are labelled correctly.
comments.explanation: This description is mathematically inconsistent with the paper's methodology. The encoder inputs are feature maps from pretrained backbones, not samples from a conditional distribution. The conditional distribution sampling applies to the network weights (q_θ(w)) and latent variables (r_ψ(z|w)), not to the backbone features. The paper's Algorithm 1 shows the backbone provides deterministic features to the encoder.

comments.title: Data augmentation description lacks technical specificity
comments.quote:
The following data augmentation methods were used to increase the size of dataset:

1. Rescaling: We rescale the pixels values by rescaling factor 1/255.
2. Rotation: Random rotations with the setup degree range between [0, 360] were used.
3. Height and Width Shift: Shifting the input to the left or right and up or down was performed.
4. Shearing Intensity: It refers to shear angle (unit in degrees) in counter-clockwise direction.
5. Brightness: It uses a brightness shift value from the setup range.
comments.explanation: The data augmentation description contains several ambiguities that prevent reproducibility. Item 1 describes pixel value rescaling by 1/255, which is actually normalization rather than augmentation that increases dataset size. Items 2-5 mention 'setup degree range' and 'setup range' without specifying the actual parameter values or ranges used. Item 3 lacks the shift magnitude or range. Item 4 doesn't provide the shear angle range. Item 5 doesn't specify the brightness shift range.

comments.title: Inconsistent KL divergence notation
comments.quote:
6.

$\mathrm{KL}(q(x))\|p(x))=-\int q(x)\log\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right)$ (6)

The
comments.explanation: Equation 6 uses inconsistent notation with parentheses placement. The KL divergence should be written as $\mathrm{KL}(q(x)\|p(x))$ with consistent bracketing. More importantly, the standard definition is $\mathrm{KL}(q\|p) = \int q(x)\log\frac{q(x)}{p(x)}dx$, which differs by a sign from what's shown. The current definition would make KL negative, contradicting the non-negativity property mentioned later.

comments.title: Wrong KL divergence direction in Equation 3
comments.quote:
uation 3.

$KL\left\{q_{\theta}(\omega)\|p(\omega|\mathcal{D})\right\}:=\int_{\Omega}q_{\theta}(\omega)\log\frac{q_{\theta}(\omega)}{p(\omega|\mathcal{D})}d\omega$ (3)

Alte
comments.explanation: The text states this measures KL divergence 'between the posterior and the true distribution' but the equation shows $KL\{q_\theta(\omega)\|p(\omega|\mathcal{D})\}$, which is KL divergence from the variational approximation TO the true posterior. In variational inference, we minimize $KL\{q_\theta(\omega)\|p(\omega|\mathcal{D})\}$, not the reverse. The description is misleading because it suggests symmetry, but KL divergence is asymmetric.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about FCN architecture
comments.quote: The main characteristic of FCN architectures is that it doesn’t use fully connected layers at the end which have been used successfully for image classification problems.
comments.explanation: This statement is misleading. FCNs do use fully connected layers internally - they simply replace the final fully connected classification layers with convolutional layers to enable dense pixel-wise predictions. The key innovation is converting classification networks to fully convolutional form, not eliminating all fully connected layers.

comments.title: Incomplete explanation of skip connections purpose
comments.quote: Skip connections connect the encoder layers to the decoder layer to create an additional path for the flow of gradients back in the backpropagation step. This helps in reducing overfitting due to many parameters involved while training the network.
comments.explanation: This explanation misses the primary purpose of skip connections. While they do help with gradient flow, their main function is to preserve spatial information and combine high-level semantic features with low-level detailed features, which is crucial for accurate segmentation. The overfitting claim is also questionable - skip connections primarily improve performance by feature fusion, not regularization.

comments.title: Misleading claim about BNN ensemble interpretation
comments.quote: Bayesian Neural Networks can be considered an ensemble of neural networks initialized randomly which averages the test results in parallel *(Gal, 2016)*.
comments.explanation: This statement mischaracterizes Bayesian Neural Networks. BNNs are not simply ensembles of randomly initialized networks; they maintain a full posterior distribution over weights given the observed data. The ensemble interpretation emerges from approximating this posterior through sampling, but the key distinction is that BNN weights are conditioned on the training data, not randomly initialized.

comments.title: Confusion about VAE solving BNN computational problems
comments.quote: Variational Autoencoder *(Kingma et al., 2015)* which is based on generative models solves the above problems and has been successful in a number of tasks like generating images, texts, recommender systems etc.
comments.explanation: This statement incorrectly claims that VAEs solve the computational problems of Bayesian CNNs. VAEs and Bayesian CNNs are different approaches: VAEs are generative models that learn latent variable representations for data generation, while Bayesian CNNs aim to learn weight distributions for uncertainty quantification in supervised tasks. VAEs don't inherently solve the computational challenges of BNNs - they address different problems entirely.

comments.title: Table reference inconsistency for DSC metrics
comments.quote:
The Mean Dice Similarity value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 2.

The IOU value for various backbone architectures compared against different train size values are shown in Table 3.
comments.explanation: There is a clear inconsistency between the text and table references. The text states DSC values are in Table 2, but Table 2 actually contains uncertainty values. Similarly, the text states IOU values are in Table 3, but Table 3 contains DSC values. The correct references should be: DSC values are shown in Table 3, and IOU values are shown in Table 4.

comments.title: Figure reference mismatch
comments.quote:
The sample from the dataset is shown in Figure 2.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)
Figure 1: Example of MRI slices and ground truth segmentation
comments.explanation: The text states that a sample is shown in Figure 2, but the actual figure caption labels it as Figure 1. This inconsistency between the text reference and figure label needs to be corrected. Either update the text to reference Figure 1, or change the figure caption to Figure 2 to match the text.

comments.title: Typo in true positive abbreviation
comments.quote: True positive (TR), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP) number of pixels is calculated separately for each image and averaged over the test set.
comments.explanation: The text uses 'TR' as the abbreviation for true positive, but the equations consistently use 'TP'. This inconsistency creates confusion between the textual description and the mathematical formulation. Rewrite 'True positive (TR), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP)' as 'True positive (TP), false negative (FN) and false positive (FP)' to match the variable names used in the equations.

comments.title: Ambiguous dropout specification
comments.quote: |  Dropout(Both Training and Test) | 0.3  |
comments.explanation: The dropout specification 'Dropout(Both Training and Test)' is ambiguous and potentially incorrect. Standard dropout is typically applied only during training and disabled during evaluation. If dropout is indeed used during both training and test time, this suggests Monte Carlo dropout for uncertainty estimation, but this should be clearly stated.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset citation
comments.quote: To validate the performance of our proposed approach to generalization, publicly available datasets were used for brain tumour segmentation BRATS18 (Menze et al., 2015) and (Bakas et al., 2018).
comments.explanation: The text mentions BRATS18 but cites two different papers. The Menze et al. (2015) reference corresponds to BRATS2015, while Bakas et al. (2018) refers to BRATS2018. The dataset description should be consistent with the citation. Since the text explicitly states BRATS18, it should only cite the Bakas et al. (2018) paper, or clarify if it's using a combined version.

comments.title: Ambiguous patient count description
comments.quote: It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma.
comments.explanation: The phrasing is ambiguous about whether there are 175 patients total with both conditions, or 175 patients with glioblastoma plus additional patients with lower grade glioblastoma. The BRATS datasets typically contain a mix of high-grade (HGG) and low-grade (LGG) glioma cases. The text should clarify the exact composition, for example: 'It contains MRI scans from 175 patients, including both high-grade glioma (HGG) and lower-grade glioma (LGG) cases.'

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a variational inference (VI) framework for brain tumor segmentation using an encoder-decoder architecture, aiming to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. While the motivation is sound, the paper suffers from significant gaps in methodological rigor, experimental validation, and clarity regarding the proposed Bayesian integration.

The authors attempt to address a relevant problem in medical imaging by applying Bayesian principles to segmentation. However, the manuscript lacks the necessary depth in its derivation of the objective function and fails to provide a convincing empirical comparison against established uncertainty quantification benchmarks.

Overview issues:

- Inconsistent and Unclear Objective Function: The derivation of the objective function in Section 3.3, specifically Equation 13, is problematic. It is unclear how the terms in the summation relate to the standard ELBO formulation provided in Section 2.3. The inclusion of an encoder $r_{\psi}(z|w)$ that maps weights to a latent space $z$ is unconventional for standard BNNs and lacks a clear theoretical justification. The authors must clarify the relationship between their specific architecture and the standard variational inference objective to ensure the model is actually performing the claimed Bayesian approximation.

- Lack of Uncertainty Calibration Analysis: The paper claims to quantify uncertainty but fails to perform standard calibration evaluations. Metrics like Expected Calibration Error (ECE) or Brier score are absent, despite being standard for validating uncertainty estimates in medical segmentation. Without these, the visual results in Figure 4 remain qualitative and do not prove that the model's uncertainty estimates are reliable or clinically meaningful. The authors should include calibration curves and quantitative metrics to demonstrate that their uncertainty estimates correlate with model error.

- Unclear Distinction Between Aleatoric and Epistemic Components: In Section 4, the authors claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are 'co-related' and show an inverse relationship in Figure 4. This observation is theoretically questionable and potentially points to a failure in the model's ability to disentangle the two. Standard Bayesian models should treat these as distinct components originating from different sources (data noise vs. model parameter uncertainty). The authors need to provide a more rigorous discussion on why their specific implementation leads to this correlation and whether it indicates a limitation in their variational approximation.

- Insufficient Comparison with State-of-the-Art Baselines: The experimental section compares the proposed method against MC dropout and ensembles but lacks a comparison with more recent, high-performing evidential deep learning or multi-decoder approaches. Given the literature context, the reported performance improvements are modest and do not clearly demonstrate the superiority of the proposed VI architecture. To be competitive, the paper needs to include a more rigorous benchmark against current state-of-the-art methods on the BRATS dataset using standardized uncertainty metrics.

- Ambiguous Role of Pre-trained Backbones: Section 3.3 mentions that the input to the encoder comes from a 'pre-trained image segmentation architecture' like U-Net. It is not clear how the weights of this pre-trained backbone are treated within the Bayesian framework. If the backbone is fixed, the epistemic uncertainty captured by the model is severely limited to the final layers, which contradicts the goal of full weight uncertainty. The authors must clarify if the entire network is being trained variationally or if they are only applying VI to a subset of the parameters.

- Absence of OOD performance validation for uncertainty reliability: The paper claims to provide a 'principled way' of measuring uncertainty, yet the evaluation is restricted to the BRATS dataset. In safety-critical medical imaging, the core value of uncertainty quantification lies in detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs or corrupted data where the model should exhibit high epistemic uncertainty. The authors should evaluate their model on a corrupted version of the BRATS dataset—such as images with added Gaussian noise, motion artifacts, or intensity shifts—to demonstrate that the epistemic uncertainty component correctly increases when the model encounters data outside its training distribution.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the KL-divergence weight: The objective function in Equation 13 combines terms that implicitly require a weighting factor (often denoted as beta) to balance the reconstruction loss and the KL-divergence term. This hyperparameter is known to be highly sensitive in variational inference, as it dictates the trade-off between the model's fit to the data and the prior distribution over weights. The paper provides no evidence that the chosen balance is robust or optimal. The authors should present a sensitivity analysis showing how varying this weight affects both the segmentation accuracy (DSC) and the quality of the uncertainty estimates, as an improper balance often leads to posterior collapse or overconfident predictions.

- Missing computational complexity and inference time analysis: The authors propose a Bayesian encoder-decoder framework and emphasize its implementation via Bayes by Backprop. However, they do not discuss the computational overhead at inference time, which is a major barrier to clinical adoption. Since the model relies on sampling from the posterior distribution, it is likely significantly slower than a standard deterministic U-Net. The paper should provide a quantitative comparison of the inference time per volume (in seconds) between their proposed method and a standard U-Net, as this is necessary for readers to assess the practical feasibility of the method in real-time clinical workflows.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a clear, mathematically consistent derivation of the objective function (Eq 13) and explain its relationship to the standard ELBO.

- Include quantitative uncertainty calibration metrics (e.g., ECE, NLL) to validate the reliability of the uncertainty estimates.

- Clarify the treatment of pre-trained weights within the Bayesian framework and explain how the model disentangles aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty.

- Add a rigorous comparison against recent state-of-the-art benchmarks in the BRATS challenge to justify the architectural choices.

comments.title: Conflicting gradient update signs
comments.quote: \theta = \theta + \alpha \nabla_ {\theta} \mathcal {L} \text { and } \psi = \psi + \beta \nabla_ {\psi}
comments.explanation: The update rule in Equation 14 uses a plus sign with the gradient. If L is a loss function, this performs gradient ascent, which is the opposite of the intended minimization. If you intend to maximize an objective like the ELBO, you must explicitly state that L is an objective to be maximized. Please clarify the nature of L and adjust the sign convention to match standard optimization practices.

comments.title: Inconsistent patient count for BRATS18
comments.quote: It contains MRI scans of 175 patients with glioblastoma and lower grade glioblastoma.
comments.explanation: The stated count of 175 patients is inconsistent with the official BRATS 2018 training dataset, which contains 285 patients. If you used a specific subset of the data, you must explicitly state this to avoid confusion with the full benchmark. Providing an incorrect count for a standard public dataset hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Logical contradiction in uncertainty correlation
comments.quote: Another point to be noted is that aleatoric uncertainty and epistemic uncertainty are co-related i.e with the increase in confidence taking into account aleatoric uncertainty while segmenting brain tumours results in increase of epistemic uncertainty and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The claim that aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties are positively correlated contradicts the standard interpretation of these components. Aleatoric uncertainty captures data noise, while epistemic uncertainty captures model ignorance; they are typically distinct. Furthermore, the phrasing 'increase in confidence... results in increase of epistemic uncertainty' is logically incoherent, as higher confidence should correspond to lower uncertainty. Please provide a theoretical justification for this observation or clarify if this indicates a failure in the model's disentanglement of the two types.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for MC Dropout
comments.quote: Monte Carlo dropout networks *(Kingma et al., 2015)* use dropout layers to approximate deep Gaussian processes
comments.explanation: The seminal paper establishing MC Dropout as a Bayesian approximation is Gal and Ghahramani (2016), not Kingma et al. (2015). Kingma et al. (2015) introduced the Variational Autoencoder and the reparameterization trick. Please update the citation to reflect the correct origin of the MC Dropout methodology.

comments.title: Inaccurate justification for skip connections
comments.quote: Skip connections connect the encoder layers to the decoder layer to create an additional path for the flow of gradients back in the backpropagation step. This helps in reducing overfitting
comments.explanation: The claim that skip connections primarily reduce overfitting is misleading. Their main purpose in U-Net architectures is to recover spatial information lost during downsampling by concatenating high-resolution feature maps. Attributing the reduction of overfitting to skip connections ignores the role of the bottleneck and the specific nature of the segmentation task. Please rewrite this to emphasize the preservation of spatial resolution.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a relevant topic by applying variational inference to biomedical image segmentation, but the execution suffers from fundamental methodological gaps, missing validation metrics, and misleading experimental results. The variational objective is mathematically incoherent, uncertainty calibration is entirely absent, and performance claims contradict established benchmarks on the BRATS dataset.

The manuscript tackles an important problem in medical AI by attempting to quantify aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. The literature review covers foundational Bayesian deep learning concepts, and the visualizations in Figure 4 demonstrate an effort to map uncertainty spatially. However, the lack of a coherent training objective, missing calibration metrics, and unstandardized experimental setup prevent the claims from being scientifically credible or clinically actionable.

Overview issues:

- Incoherent Variational Objective and Segmentation Loss Integration: The mathematical formulation in Section 2.3 and Equation 13 mixes variational autoencoder reconstruction terms with weight posteriors, yet omits the likelihood term for the segmentation task. Section 3.5 then introduces a standard Dice and binary cross-entropy loss (Equation 18) without explaining how it connects to the variational objective. This creates a fundamental disconnect: the paper claims to learn a posterior distribution over weights via variational inference, but the training procedure relies on a deterministic segmentation loss. Readers cannot verify whether the weight distributions are actually optimized or merely initialized and frozen. The authors must explicitly derive a joint objective that couples the ELBO with the segmentation task, or clearly state that the variational components are fixed priors. Without a coherent loss formulation, the claimed Bayesian framework collapses into a standard U-Net with auxiliary sampling.

- Absence of Uncertainty Calibration and Quantitative Validation: The manuscript reports only Dice and IoU scores while claiming to provide a principled uncertainty quantification method. Medical imaging UQ literature requires calibration metrics like Expected Calibration Error, Negative Log-Likelihood, or reliability diagrams to demonstrate that predicted uncertainty aligns with actual error rates. Table 2 lists mean uncertainty values for MC dropout and deep ensembles but provides no thresholding strategy, decision-curve analysis, or correlation with segmentation failures. Figure 4 displays aleatoric and epistemic maps, yet offers no quantitative proof that these maps highlight anatomical boundaries or out-of-distribution regions. Adding calibration plots and error-uncertainty correlation analyses is essential to move beyond qualitative visualization and establish clinical relevance.

- Unsubstantiated Performance Claims and Non-Standard Experimental Protocol: The abstract states the model outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods, but Tables 3 and 4 report Dice scores between 0.53 and 0.64. Published benchmarks on the BRATS dataset consistently achieve Dice scores above 0.85, making this claim factually incorrect. The experimental setup uses training sizes of 5, 10, 15, and 20 samples, which deviates sharply from standard BRATS protocols and prevents meaningful comparison with existing literature. Figure 3 plots an undefined accuracy versus data distribution curve that lacks axes labels and statistical context. The authors need to clarify the few-shot intent, run experiments on standard train/val/test splits, and remove the state-of-the-art claim until the results are benchmarked correctly.

- Missing Methodology Structure and Algorithmic Inconsistencies: Section 3 is completely empty, forcing readers to piece together the architecture and training procedure from scattered subsections and Algorithm 1. The algorithm samples weights and latent variables but then applies a separate gradient update for the decoder parameters (Equation 15) that resembles a standard VAE reconstruction step rather than a segmentation update. This computational graph is mathematically ambiguous and does not match standard variational dropout or Bayes-by-Backprop implementations. The paper also applies 0 to 360 degree rotations during augmentation, which is clinically unrealistic for axial brain MRI slices and likely distorts anatomical priors. A complete rewrite of the methodology section is necessary to present a reproducible, anatomically sound pipeline.

- Superficial Treatment of Aleatoric and Epistemic Disentanglement: Section 2.4 presents the variance decomposition formula from Kendall and Gal (2017) but never explains how the network architecture or training procedure actually separates the two uncertainty types. The method description lacks details on the output head, the number of Monte Carlo samples used at inference, or how the variance terms are aggregated across the feature map. Figure 4 claims the uncertainties are correlated, yet provides no ablation study to verify whether the epistemic component genuinely decreases with more training data or whether the aleatoric component captures label noise. The authors should specify the exact mechanism for disentanglement, report sample sizes for MC sampling, and include an ablation that isolates each uncertainty component under controlled perturbations.

- Demonstration of uncertainty utility on segmentation errors: The paper asserts that its model provides a principled measure of uncertainty, yet it never validates whether this uncertainty actually helps in practice. Readers will wonder how a clinical pipeline should use the predicted maps. Without showing that high uncertainty correlates with actual segmentation failures, the quantification remains an unverified byproduct. The authors should compute the pixel-wise correlation between the predicted variance and ground-truth errors on the BRATS test set. Adding a reliability diagram would concretely demonstrate whether the model flags ambiguous regions effectively.

- Worked example for the hybrid VAE-segmentation objective: Readers will struggle to follow the joint optimization landscape because the theoretical foundation mixes variational autoencoder reconstruction terms with a deterministic Dice loss. A natural question is whether the weight posteriors actually contribute to the segmentation likelihood or merely act as regularizers. Computing the full objective for a simple 2D Gaussian mixture segmentation task would let readers calibrate their intuitions about the trade-off between the KL divergence and the segmentation loss. This worked example should explicitly show how the variational posterior updates the predictive distribution for pixel labels.

- Inference protocol and computational overhead analysis: Reproducibility suffers because the inference protocol remains underspecified. The paper relies on Monte Carlo sampling to estimate uncertainty but omits the practical details required for clinical deployment. Readers need to know exactly how many forward passes are performed at test time, how the stochastic predictions are aggregated, and what the memory footprint looks like compared to a standard deterministic U-Net. A concrete implementation discussion would specify the number of MC samples, the hardware setup, and the per-slice inference latency. Without this, the claim of scalability cannot be verified.
